description_long: This gate consists of two adjacent panels constructed from vertically aligned, weathered wooden planks, each supported by a simple metal frame. One panel features a rectangular cut-out window, while the other is solid, providing a visual contrast and potential for controlled visibility or communication. The wood appears aged and darkened, with visible grain and some irregularities, suggesting outdoor use and exposure to the elements. The supporting legs and horizontal bars are made of metal, likely steel, providing structural stability and resistance to bending. The overall design is utilitarian, with no visible decorative elements, focusing on function over form. The gate is likely intended for temporary or semi-permanent access control in urban environments such as construction sites, alleyways, or event perimeters.
description: A double-panel wooden gate with metal supports, featuring one solid panel and one with a rectangular window. The design is utilitarian and weathered, suitable for urban access control.
description_view_0: A side view showing the thin profile of the gate and the angled metal support legs.
description_view_1: A rear view displaying both wooden panels, with the left panel featuring a rectangular window and the right panel being solid.
description_view_2: The opposite side view, again highlighting the narrowness and the staggered support structure.
description_view_3: A front view showing the full face of both panels, clearly revealing the window cut-out on the left panel.
